← jmyteves.github.io

BEACON — pan-cancer

BEACON is a model-based prediction tool for compact bidirectional promoters (BDPs), their co-expression across 14 cancers, and the predicted properties of their co-driver.

What is a compact BDP? A compact BDP is a pair of protein-coding genes arranged head-to-head with their transcription start sites less than 1 kb apart, so both genes are driven from one shared, nucleosome-depleted regulatory region. This tight architecture couples the two genes: they tend to be co-expressed, and the arrangement is deeply conserved across evolution. The current collection has (505 Caco-2-validated + 287 expansion pack)

Co-driver quadrant — BDPs (click a point)

x = dosage coupling (CN→expr ρ) · y = coordinated output (arm–arm ρ) · color = amplification prevalence · shaded = co-driver zone (dosage ρ≥0.3) · □ open = expansion pack. Selected BDP ringed.

Browse all BDPs (click row; sort by column)

We previously found that compact BDPs are important in colorectal cancer (Email me to learn more →). One compact BDP in particular, PLAGL2–POFUT1, is a textbook 108-bp head-to-head promoter within the 20q11.21 locus that couples the expression of PLAGL2 (a Wnt driver) and POFUT1 (a Notch modifier). In colorectal cancer these two genes are the most tightly co-expressed of the BDP pairs we tested (Li et al. 2019). Their coupling is associated with a constitutive architectural property of the chromatin conformation (measured by Hi-C), and 20q copy-number amplification co-drives their shared output. This relationship can be modelled as a simple feed-forward loop. From that model we identified a transcription-factor motif, PRDM15, enriched within the chromatin neighborhood of the PLAGL2–POFUT1 BDP, one of the co-drivers associated with colorectal tumors.

We then asked whether this property generalises to other compact BDPs and other cancers. That is how BEACON was conceived. For any compact bidirectional promoter in any of 14 cancers, BEACON predicts whether copy-number amplification acts as a coherent feed-forward co-driver of the promoter’s coordinated output, and returns four properties of a candidate co-driver (like PRDM15) that you can test and validate in the lab for your cancer of interest. BEACON is deployed as a portable, open-source pan-cancer template, the mechanism we characterised and expanded from colorectal cancer.

Why do BDPs matter in cancer? When a cell divides uncontrollably, a stretch of its genome can be duplicated in a process called copy-number amplification (CNA). This raises both genes that share one control region together. When a compact BDP couples two cancer-relevant genes, that amplification can co-drive a coordinated oncogenic output a single-gene view would miss. BEACON provides a comprehensive pan-cancer list of these BDPs and their potential co-drivers, offering a new way to view transcriptional regulation in cancer.

References. Li et al. 2019, EBioMedicine 45:124–138 (doi:10.1016/j.ebiom.2019.06.051), the PLAGL2–POFUT1 anchor. Thompson, Christensen & Marsit 2018, Int J Mol Sci 19:2296 (doi:10.3390/ijms19082296), pan-cancer BDP susceptibility. Chen et al. 2021, Front Genet 11:560997 (doi:10.3389/fgene.2020.560997), pan-cancer head-to-head gene pairs.

Predictions are measured associations from TCGA PanCancer Atlas 2018 (linear coordinated scaling & GISTIC copy-number coupling), shown with effect size — hypotheses to test, not a fitted score. Expansion pack = compact BDPs silent/untestable in Caco-2 but architecturally defined, included to remove cell-line ascertainment bias. Companion to the PLAGL2–POFUT1 study & the compact-BDP atlas.

Created by Dr. Joji Marie Teves, PhD · jmyteves@gmail.com · github.com/jmyteves/beacon · jmyteves.github.io
